sources.notes: Statement and hacks format: originally interactive, but hacks provide the hidden binary string as standard input.
Used to confirm f(l,r)=m/gcd(m, number_of_ones), parity extraction on even-length queries, and the two-ended low-cost reconstruction.
Used to cross-check the closed form for f and the query-graph viewpoint.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Reconstruct a hidden binary string using queries that return the number of distinct inversion residues over cyclic rotations of a substring; in hacks the hidden string is supplied as input.
annotation.constraints: Sum n <= 3000 and the intended query cost is bounded by O(n), but the underlying reconstruction idea is independent of the hack implementation.
annotation.core_idea: For a substring of length m with k ones, cyclic rotations change the inversion count by k modulo m, so the number of distinct residues is m / gcd(m,k). For even m, the parity of gcd(m,k) tells the parity of k. Thus even-length queries reveal xor/parity constraints on substrings. Starting from an assumed first bit, one can reconstruct adjacent bits using differences of prefix/suffix parity queries; querying from both ends keeps the total cost below the bound. Equivalently, build a graph of parity constraints between prefix xor states and DFS it; the complement string gives the same answers, so up to two guesses are needed.
annotation.complexity: O(n) oracle queries/cost up to the allowed constant factor; O(n) reconstruction work.
tricks: even-length query reveals parity of ones
binary string is only determined up to complement

solution_variants.name: two-ended-parity-reconstruction
solution_variants.summary: Use even-length prefix/suffix queries to infer adjacent-bit parity from known previous bits while choosing the cheaper side.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n) query cost under the intended bound

solution_variants.name: parity-constraint-graph
solution_variants.summary: Create graph edges between prefix-xor states from chosen even-length queries, then DFS/BFS from assumed initial parity to recover the string up to complement.

tags.evidence: The core task is reconstructing an unknown binary string from query answers; hacks only simulate the oracle offline.
The closed form f(l,r)=m/gcd(m,k) is the main mathematical observation.
Even-length queries are useful because they reveal the parity of the number of ones in the substring.
The intended low-cost implementation chooses prefix or suffix queries from both ends depending on which side is cheaper.
An alternative constructs a graph of parity constraints and recovers prefix xor values with DFS/BFS.
The query answers are unchanged by complementing the whole binary string, so the solution may need two guesses.
tags.description: Problems where an object and its bitwise/color/combinatorial complement are indistinguishable or symmetrically valid, and the solution must handle that ambiguity.
tags.created_reason: The existing symmetry tag is broad; complement ambiguity is a reusable searchable subpattern in binary reconstruction and coloring problems.
